title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT both equal 8, making them indistinguishable in any bitmask test
description: Line 551 defines `OUT_FULL_16 = 8` and line 556 defines `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 8`. Every bitmask test (`output_flags & OUT_FULL_16 != 0`) is identical to (`output_flags & OUT_NO_ORIENT != 0`), so callers cannot request full-16 output without also skipping orientation, or vice versa. The public API doc at line 953 says 'Pass 7 for classic. For M3 16-bit big levels pass e.g. 15 (7|8)', confirming callers expect flag 8 to mean OUT_FULL_16, but process_orf_impl (line 743) and process_dng_impl (line 1825) both check `OUT_FULL_16` for the 16-bit buffer while the OUT_NO_ORIENT guard at line 801 checks the same bit, silently also skipping orientation for every OUT_FULL_16 caller.

title: Flipflop bench mutates rgb16 in-place so subsequent flip rounds blend already-blended data, not a fresh baseline
description: The flip branch (rounds 0, 2, 4, 6) applies the NR blend `o + (b - o) * s` in-place to `rgb16` (line 490). Each subsequent flip round therefore starts from the output of the previous round rather than fresh source data. After 4 flip iterations the array has converged toward `blurred` by factor `(1 - s*(1 - s*(1 - s*(1 - s))))`, not `s`. The median across 4 such rounds measures diverging work and will give artificially low timings for later rounds as the data converges. To get a fair per-call benchmark, `rgb16` must be restored to its original state before each timed invocation.

title: meanLuma is divided by 256 but luma values reach 65025, producing an unnormalized result in [0, ~254] instead of [0, 1]
description: In `fs_to_js` the luma accumulation uses `l = 54*r + 183*g + 18*b` (weights sum to 255), so per-pixel luma spans 0..=65025 for 8-bit RGBA. `mean` is the average of these per-pixel values. Line 2375 then computes `mean / 256.0`. The resulting `meanLuma` field will lie in [0, ~254] rather than [0, 1] as callers likely expect for a normalized mean luma. `lumaVariance` (line 2362) divides by 65536, which is also inconsistent with the luma domain. The correct normalization for `meanLuma` should be `mean / 65280.0` (the maximum, 255*255) or equivalently divide the per-pixel luma first by 255*255 before summing.

title: fs_core_simd tail path resets a_min/a_max to sentinel only after a px==0 guard that is never true at that point
description: In `fs_core_simd` (wasm32 version), the `if px == 0 { a_min = 255; a_max = 0; }` guard at line 2593 is placed after the tail loop. However by that point a_min and a_max have already been set from the SIMD vmin/vmax extraction immediately above (lines 2557-2570). If px==0 then chunks==0 so vmin stays at the initialized splat(255) and vmax stays at splat(0), and extracting lane 3 from these gives 255 and 0 respectively, so the guard is correct in result — but it is doing redundant work. In the SIMD accumulator path (chunks > 0) the tail loop further updates a_min/a_max. The real issue is that if px==0 the vmin/vmax extraction loop at lines 2558-2570 still runs (extracting from splat(255)/splat(0)), setting a_min=255, a_max=0, then the tail loop skips (chunks*4==px==0), then the guard reassigns the same values. This is harmless dead work but confirms the guard is a copy-paste from `fs_core_fast` that was not reconciled with the SIMD init path.

title: DNG/CR2 ProcessResult uses exposure_den=1 / fnumber_den=1 / focal_length_den=1 as absent-sentinel, but ORF path uses 0 (breaking JS consumers that check den==0)
description: In `process_dng_impl` (and therefore `process_cr2_impl` via the shared path) at lines 1930-1935, missing rational EXIF values are filled with numerator=0 / denominator=1. The ORF path (`process_orf_impl`, lines 842-848) uses denominator=0 as the absent sentinel. The code comment on line 841 documents '0/0 as absent-sentinel (JS checks den==0 before dividing)'. JS consumers using `result.exposure_den === 0` to detect absent exposure will incorrectly treat DNG/CR2 images as having exposure 0/1 = 0 rather than absent, and may display '0 s' instead of no exposure value. The correct sentinel for DNG/CR2 should also be 0.

title: time_fn median index ms[runs/2] is the upper-middle element for even runs counts, not the true median
description: For `runs = 5`, `runs/2 = 2` (integer division), which is the middle element of a 0-indexed 5-element sorted array — correct. However the function is not restricted to odd run counts. For an even number of runs (e.g., if a caller passes `runs=4`), `ms[runs/2]` is `ms[2]` which is the upper-middle element; the true median of 4 elements is `(ms[1]+ms[2])/2`. The main() call uses `runs=5` so this does not cause a bug in practice, but the generality is broken and the `med` label in the output would be misleading for even values.

title: No compile-time or runtime assertion that output flag constants are distinct
description: The OUT_* constants (lines 548-556) are hand-assigned u32 values. The current collision between OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT (both = 8) was silently accepted by the compiler. A `const _: () = assert!(OUT_FULL_16 != OUT_NO_ORIENT && OUT_FULL_RGB8 != OUT_LIGHTBOX ...);` guard evaluated at compile time would catch any future or existing collision at zero runtime cost. This is a structural gap that allowed the existing HIGH severity bug to be undetected.
